briefs.html:
As Washington heads into the July Fourth holiday weekend, the capital's political atmosphere is shaped by a series of major developments spanning the courts, Congress, foreign policy, and the military. The past 72 hours have brought a landmark Supreme Court ruling, an unresolved standoff over the housing bill, tense Iran ceasefire diplomacy, and a rare act of public dissent by an active-duty officer.

Supreme Court Upholds Birthright Citizenship

On June 30, 2026, the Supreme Court struck down President Donald Trump's executive order and upheld the constitutional guarantee of birthright citizenship. In its 6-to-3 decision, the court struck down the president's 2025 executive order, which sought to strip citizenship from American children born to undocumented parents. The case framed another test of Trump's assertions of executive power that defy long-standing precedent for a court with a conservative majority and a robust view of presidential power that has largely ruled in his favor. Trump reacted furiously to the decision, saying he was ashamed of the justices who ruled against him and calling them unpatriotic.

Housing Bill Standoff: Clock Is Ticking

After months of negotiations, Congress passed its largest housing bill in decades — the 21st Century Road to Housing Act, co-written and championed by Senator Elizabeth Warren — with massive bipartisan majorities in both the House and Senate. The bill passed the House in a 358-32 vote and the Senate 85-5. However, President Trump abruptly refused to sign it. Trump downplayed the bill and then canceled its signing, saying he won't act on the legislation until Congress approves the controversial election measure known as the SAVE America Act. The SAVE America Act is meant to cut down on noncitizen voting in U.S. elections — which happens rarely and is already illegal in federal contests — and impose nationwide voter ID laws. Speaker Mike Johnson formally transmitted the housing bill to the White House, triggering a 10-day clock — excluding Sundays — for Trump to either sign or veto the bill; if he does neither, it will become law without his signature by July 10. Democrats and voting rights advocates have opposed voter ID laws, arguing the SAVE America Act will present hurdles that will make it harder for people to vote.

Iran Ceasefire Talks Continue in Doha

The U.S. and Iran signed a memorandum of understanding on June 17, extending their ceasefire by 60 days, during which they agreed to continue more detailed negotiations on their differences — with the future of the Strait of Hormuz, Iran's frozen assets, long-term sanctions relief, and Tehran's nuclear programme among the issues on the table. Qatari Prime Minister and Foreign Minister Sheikh Mohammed bin Abdulrahman bin Jassim Al Thani met United States envoy Steve Witkoff and President Donald Trump's son-in-law Jared Kushner in Doha as attempts are made to restart direct talks between Tehran and Washington. Vice President JD Vance told CNN that as technical negotiators sit down with the Iranians and Qataris, "we're worried about the nuclear issue, we're going to start talking about that, so right now the talks are going well."

Washington heads into the July Fourth recess with the weight of a landmark Supreme Court ruling, unresolved trade uncertainty, and a stalled housing bill hanging over the capital â all set against the broader backdrop of a looming midterm election now just four months away.

Supreme Court Upholds Birthright Citizenship

In a sharp rebuke to President Trump, the Supreme Court ruled Tuesday that the Constitution guarantees automatic birthright citizenship to virtually all children born in the United States. The court ruled 6â3 against Trump's executive order, effectively upholding a more than century-old interpretation of birthright citizenship. Chief Justice John Roberts wrote the court's opinion, citing both the colonists' demands for the "rights of Englishmen" as well as the abolitionists' lauding of the "ancient and universal" rule of citizenship by birth alone.

Justice Brett Kavanaugh agreed that Trump's order was invalid but argued it violated a federal statute rather than the Constitution itself, suggesting Congress "could amend" that law "or otherwise enact new legislation establishing exceptions to birthright citizenship." Trump called the ruling "too bad for our country" and urged Congress to act, writing: "Congress should start TODAY to work on ending expensive and unfair to our Country, Birthright Citizenship."

U.S. Declines to Renew USMCA â Annual Review Process Begins

The Trump administration decided not to renew its trilateral trade pact with Canada and Mexico, instead opting to conduct annual reviews of the treaty that President Donald Trump once called "the best agreement we've ever made." The USMCA's first mandatory Joint Review fell on July 1, 2026; the U.S., Mexico, and Canada met virtually, and the United States did not agree to renew the USMCA in its current form â meaning the agreement is not renewed.

The United States said it will continue to engage with Mexico and Canada to address the agreement's shortcomings and trade deficits, while the agreement itself remains in force pending resolution of those issues or until formal termination. If an extension is not agreed upon but the agreement is not terminated, the USMCA will move into a period of annual reviews until either an agreement is reached or it terminates in July 2036. Business groups including the U.S. Chamber of Commerce and the Business Roundtable have pushed to strengthen and retain the agreement, with one analyst warning that "supply chains are built with 30-year visibility, not five, and uncertainty could dissuade investment and growth."

Housing Bill Remains in Limbo as Voter ID Standoff Continues

The 21st Century Road to Housing Act â which aims to boost housing affordability and development â passed Congress with overwhelming bipartisan support, clearing the House in a 358-to-32 vote and the Senate in an 85-to-5 vote, but it remains unsigned. President Trump canceled a planned signing ceremony for the legislation in an effort to pressure Republicans to pass the SAVE America Act, a bill that would require voters to provide documentary proof of U.S. citizenship and strict photo ID to vote in federal elections. Senate Majority Leader John Thune has faced the difficult task of advancing the voter ID measure through a chamber where it has struggled to attract the Democratic support needed to clear a filibuster, leaving both bills in an unresolved standoff as lawmakers depart for the holiday recess.

The housing legislation would reduce federal regulations, streamline environmental reviews, and expand access to affordable development financing â benefits that supporters argue are being held hostage to a separate partisan dispute over election law.
